question: You split a monolith into ten services. What problem is an API gateway primarily there to solve?
options: It makes each service's database queries faster
It centralizes cross-cutting edge concerns (TLS, auth, rate limiting, routing) so every service doesn't reimplement them
It removes the need to write the services at all
It stores each user's session in memory so services can be stateless
explanation: A gateway is one front door for concerns that belong to no single service — TLS termination, authentication, rate limiting, routing, observability. Doing them once at the edge stops ten services from drifting into ten slightly-different copies, and hides the topology from clients.

question: A gateway and a service mesh both operate at L7. What's the difference in what they govern?
options: They are two names for the same thing
The gateway governs north–south traffic (client ↔ system); the mesh governs east–west traffic (service ↔ service)
The gateway is for databases; the mesh is for caches
The mesh replaces the gateway in modern systems
explanation: A gateway sits at the north–south edge between clients and your system. A service mesh (via per-pod sidecars) handles east–west calls between services inside the cluster — mTLS, retries, tracing. Most systems run both; they complement rather than replace each other.

question: What is a Backend for Frontend (BFF)?
options: A load balancer placed in front of the database
A thin, frontend-team-owned backend that aggregates shared services and shapes the payload for one specific client
A caching layer shared by all clients
The single general-purpose API every client must use
explanation: A BFF is a thin backend tailored to one frontend (web, iOS, partner). It aggregates the shared services and returns exactly the shape that client needs — lean for mobile, wide for a dashboard — so a frontend team can iterate on its own contract. Keep it thin: aggregation and shaping, never business logic.

question: A BFF endpoint must combine data from three downstream services into one response. What's the right way to call them?
options: In series, one after another, so failures are easy to trace
In parallel, with a timeout on each call and a deliberate partial-failure policy
In a single database JOIN across all three services
Only the first one; skip the rest to stay fast
explanation: Fan out in parallel so the response is as slow as the slowest downstream, not the sum of all three. Put a timeout on each call and decide per field what happens on failure — fail the screen, or degrade gracefully (e.g. render the order with product names omitted) rather than hang on the slowest dependency.

question: Your team keeps moving pricing and order-validation logic into the API gateway because 'it's already in the request path.' What's the risk?
options: None — centralizing logic in the gateway is the goal
The gateway becomes a God-object / distributed monolith (the ESB anti-pattern) that every team must coordinate on to deploy
The gateway will run out of disk space
Clients will need to send more headers
explanation: Business logic in the gateway recreates the Enterprise Service Bus mistake: a central component every team must coordinate on, coupling all their deploys. Keep the gateway to policy and routing; domain logic belongs in the services it fronts.

question: "The gateway already authenticates every request, so my orders-service can trust any caller it receives." Why is this wrong?
options: It's correct — once the gateway checks auth, downstream services need no checks
Defense in depth: an attacker inside the network or a misrouted internal call must still be rejected, so services still authorize access to their own data
Because the gateway can't actually verify tokens
Because services should re-terminate TLS on every call
explanation: The gateway removes the duplication of the edge check — it doesn't remove each service's duty to guard its own data. An internal attacker, a bug, or a misrouted call can reach a service without passing the gateway, so services still enforce authorization. The gateway injects a trusted identity; the service still decides what that identity may do.
